explanation_md: `reserve(5)` sets capacity to at least 5 but does not add any elements — size stays 0. The two `push_back` calls bring size to 2. Capacity (≥5) is not printed.

prompt_md:
After calling `v.resize(3)` on a vector that previously had 10 elements, which statement is true?

a) `v.size()` is 10 and `v.capacity()` is 3
b) `v.size()` is 3 and `v.capacity()` is at least 3 (typically still 10)
c) `v.size()` is 3 and `v.capacity()` is guaranteed to be exactly 3
d) `v.size()` is 0 because resize clears the vector

options.b: `v.size()` is 3 and `v.capacity()` is at least 3 (typically still 10)
options.c: `v.size()` is 3 and `v.capacity()` is guaranteed to be exactly 3
options.d: `v.size()` is 0 because resize clears the vector

explanation_md: `resize` changes size to the requested count and keeps (at least) the existing capacity — shrinking does not free memory. The standard does not require capacity to decrease, so option (c) is wrong. Option (d) is entirely wrong; resize never clears the vector.
The vector starts as {7,7,7,7}. `resize(6)` grows it to {7,7,7,7,0,0} (new elements value-initialised). `resize(2)` shrinks it to {7,7}. Printing indices 0 and 1 gives 7 7, and `size()` is 2.

prompt_md:
What is the primary purpose of `v.reserve(n)` on a `std::vector`?

a) It sets the number of elements in the vector to `n`, filling new slots with 0
b) It prevents the vector from ever growing beyond `n` elements
c) It pre-allocates memory for at least `n` elements without changing the element count
d) It copies the first `n` elements of the vector into a new allocation

options.a: It sets the number of elements in the vector to `n`, filling new slots with 0
options.b: It prevents the vector from ever growing beyond `n` elements
options.c: It pre-allocates memory for at least `n` elements without changing the element count
options.d: It copies the first `n` elements of the vector into a new allocation

explanation_md: `reserve` is purely a performance hint: it ensures the capacity is at least `n` so subsequent `push_back` calls up to that count will not cause reallocations. Size is unchanged. It imposes no upper bound on growth (option b is wrong), and it is not `resize` (option a).

options.a: `reserve` is not allowed on a default-constructed vector
options.b: `v[0]` is an out-of-bounds access because `reserve` does not add elements
options.c: The vector should use `v.at(0)` instead of `v[0]` after `reserve`
options.d: Assigning through `operator[]` is not allowed on `int` elements

explanation_md: `reserve(10)` allocates memory but does not add any elements — `v.size()` is still 0. Accessing `v[0]` is out of bounds and undefined behaviour. To make this work, call `v.resize(1)` (or `v.push_back(42)`) before the subscript access.
